title: Extending ACT-R to Tackle Deceptive Overgeneralization in Intelligent Tutoring Systems
authors: An M.
venue: CEUR Workshop Proceedings
abstract: This research extends the ACT-R cognitive architecture to tackle deceptive overgeneralization within Intelligent Tutoring Systems (ITS). Existing adaptive learning technologies, while effective, rely on learning data that may not fully capture the nuances of learner understanding, particularly in cases of deceptive overgeneralization. This phenomenon occurs when learners exhibit correct actions during monitored learning sessions, yet these actions are grounded in an incomplete understanding of the necessary conditions. Due to the reliance on observed correctness, ITS may falsely assess mastery, potentially ceasing to provide further necessary practice opportunities that could aid in the refinement of understanding. This study aims to identify ITS designs that may inadvertently foster such misconceptions and to develop methods for their detection, diagnosis, and correction. Utilizing experimental designs, think-aloud protocols, and educational data mining, the research seeks to refine the adaptivity of ITS and enable more accurate assessments of true skill mastery. This work contributes to Technology-Enhanced Learning (TEL) by enhancing the precision of automated assessments and supporting more reliable adaptive learning experiences.  © 2024 Copyright for this paper by its authors.

title: Evaluating Sampling Strategies for Similarity-Based Short Answer Scoring: a Case Study in Thailand
authors: Boonsarngsuk P.; Arpanantikul P.; Hiranwipas S.; Watcharakajorn W.; Chuangsuwanich E.
venue: Proceedings - International Conference on Computational Linguistics, COLING
abstract: Automatic short answer scoring is a task whose aim is to help grade written works by learners of some subject matter. In niche subject domains with small examples, existing methods primarily utilized similarity-based scoring, relying on predefined reference answers to grade each student’s answer based on the similarity to the reference. However, these reference answers are often generated from a randomly selected set of graded student answer, which may fail to represent the full range of scoring variations. We propose a semi-automatic scoring framework that enhances the selective sampling strategy for defining the reference answers through a K-center-based and a K-means-based sampling method. Our results demonstrate that our framework outperforms previous similarity-based scoring methods on a dataset with Thai and English. Moreover, it achieves competitive performance compared to human reference performance and LLMs. © 2025 Association for Computational Linguistics.

title: Trojsten Benchmark: Evaluating LLM Problem-Solving in Slovak STEM Competition Problems
authors: Zahradník A.; Šuppa M.
venue: EMNLP 2025 - 2025 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing, Proceedings of the Conference
abstract: Large language models show promising performance on reasoning tasks, yet evaluation methods for low-resource languages remain limited, particularly for complex STEM problem-solving. We introduce Trojsten Benchmark, a Slovak-language dataset of 1,108 high-school competition problems with reference solutions across mathematics, physics, and programming, and a rubric-based LLM grading framework. Using GPT-4 to generate rubrics and grade solutions, we observe 1.05 average absolute deviation from human graders (5-point scale), while benchmarking GPT-3.5-Turbo, GPT-4, GPT-4o, and open-weight models (Llama 3, Phi-3). We quantify multistep reasoning performance by difficulty, show consistent underperformance on harder items, and demonstrate language sensitivity: accuracy drops on English translations of Slovak statements, evidencing challenges beyond translation. Trojsten Benchmark complements English-centric math datasets (e.g., MATH, GSM8K) by targeting open-response, rubric-gradable reasoning under low-resource linguistic framing. We release code and data to enable reproducible evaluation and human-aligned auto-grading for STEM in under-served languages. © 2025 Association for Computational Linguistics.

title: The Effect of Artificial Intelligence on Enhancing Education Quality and Reduce the Levels of Future Anxiety among Jordanian Teachers
authors: Beirat M.A.; Tashtoush D.M.; Khasawneh M.A.S.; Az-Zo’bi E.A.; Tashtoush M.A.
venue: Applied Mathematics and Information Sciences
abstract: As it embarks on its digital transformation journey and strives for ambitious education changes, Jordan faces two critical issues: teacher burnout and the incorporation of technology. This cross-sectional survey research aimed to discover how burned out secondary school teachers were, how confident they were in technology, and how they felt about the incorporation of AI. Stratified random sampling was used to choose a subset of the 893 educators in Irbid province. A worrisome 20.3 mean feeling emotionally drained score, indicating a burnout risk, was found using validated scales in a study of 57 educators. Encouraging digital education platforms (2.99 mean) and administration and resolution of technological problems (2.88 mean) were areas in which instructors reported less proficiency, while they were relatively competent in basic usage of general technology (3.65 mean). Human control was still highly valued, but there was cautious optimism about AI’s ability to computerize grading (68.4% positive), automated administration tasks (78.8% good), and customized education (76.1% positive). The regression analysis found that experience and gender had a significant impact on burnout and technology confidence, emphasizing the importance of professional development. The qualitative results highlighted the practical factors that contribute to teachers’ stress. The study’s implications encompass policies that prioritize teachers, integrate AI to automate administrative tasks to a limited extent, and involve teachers in the digital transition as partners who prioritize student welfare over test scores. Additionally, the study suggests conducting localized research to inform solutions that are appropriate to specific contexts. © 2025 NSP

title: Automated Grading of Discussion Posts in Online Courses
authors: Dumoulin G.; Sakeef N.; Dewan M.A.A.; Wen D.
venue: Proceedings - 2025 IEEE Smart World Congress, SWC 2025, 2025 IEEE Ubiquitous Intelligence and Computing, Autonomous and Trusted Computing, Digital Twin, Metaverse, Scalable Computing and Communications
abstract: Discussion forums and online courses have been growing in popularity due to recent advancements in education and technology. However, the means of effectively managing these tools have not been fully developed. This paper proposes a method for automatic grading of students' posts in course discussion forums as an alternative approach to manual evaluation. The method employs a machine learning based approach to analyze students' posts on three key aspects: engagement, post relevance, and writing quality. The aspects are analyzed individually to enhance the model's precision and combined to provide instructors with a flexible grading system. The auto grading system is developed using GloVe embeddings, Sentence-BERT, and Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF) along with other textual features and machine learning model. This system is developed with the intend to reduce instructors' workload, provide students with self-assessment opportunities, and enhance their comprehension and engagement in courses. © 2025 IEEE.

title: Beyond Chatbots: Enhancing Luxembourgish Language Learning Through Multi-agent Systems and Large Language Model
authors: Nouzri S.; EL Fatimi M.; Guerin T.; Othmane M.; Najjar A.
venue: Lecture Notes in Computer Science (including subseries Lecture Notes in Artificial Intelligence and Lecture Notes in Bioinformatics)
abstract: The intersection of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and education is transforming learning and teaching, with Generative AI (GenAI) and large language models (LLMs) offering new possibilities. AI and LLMs personalize learning through adaptive study guides, instant feedback, automated grading, and content creation, making resources more accessible and tailored to individual needs. Notably, LLM-based chatbots, such as OpenAI’s ChatGPT, serve as virtual assistants, ideal for language practice. However, these chatbots often limit themselves to teaching vocabulary through role-playing conversations or providing instant feedback based on model-generated content, which may lead to exposure to inaccuracies. This overlooks the holistic nature of Language Learning (LL), which requires pedagogy, effective methods, reliable content, and a supportive teacher-student relationship. Therefore, relying on a single chatbot is inefficient for the entire learning process. In this paper, a Multi-Agent System (MAS) is proposed, where each agent specializes in a specific function, working together to provide personalized, adaptive learning support. This approach breaks down the complex learning process into manageable parts. It employs the Business Process Model and Notation (BPMN), translated into agent-based modeling and LLMs to create dynamic, tailored learning environments. By simulating interactions similar to human tutoring, this model ensures real-time adjustments to meet each student’s evolving needs. Our project aims to address these limitations by using LL books with robust pedagogical resources as primary references. We focus on teaching Luxembourgish, adding complexity to our challenges as it is a low-resource language, ensuring a holistic learning experience. Our approach employs complex LLM workflows as multi-agent collaborations for reading, conversing, listening, and mastering grammar, based on GPT-4o, enhanced by Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and voice recognition features. © The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2025.

title: Unraveling Cocaine and Heroin Addiction Patterns: A Robust LLM Approach Using iRISA Elements to Analyze Short and Spontaneous Speech Samples
authors: Agurto C.; Wen B.; Rogers J.L.; King S.; Alia-Klein N.; Cecchi G.A.; Goldstein R.Z.
venue: Proceedings - 2025 IEEE International Conference on Digital Health, ICDH 2025
abstract: This work presents a robust method for characterizing addiction patterns in individuals with cocaine and heroin use disorders by analyzing short, spontaneous speech samples using a large language model (LLM) framework. This architecture is designed to identify key elements of the Impaired Response Inhibition and Salience Attribution (iRISA) theoretical model and use this framework to assess whether features related to iRISA can inform drug use metrics, without directly evaluating the models’ inference of iRISA elements against human gradings. Specifically, i RISA c aptures d isruptions i n self-regulation, inhibitory control, and the attribution of salience to drug-related versus non-drug-related stimuli. Our analysis revealed significant correlations between iRISA elements and substance use patterns, with these associations being more pronounced when individuals talk about the positive consequences of quitting drugs (PC) than the negative consequences of drug use (NC). Prediction models utilizing outputs from the iRISA LLM framework demonstrated that integrating features from both PC and NC significantly improved predictive accuracy, especially for variables such as days of abstinence (r up to 0.58), withdrawal symptoms (r up to 0.41), and dependence severity (r up to 0.42). These findings highlight the potential of this approach to provide in-depth, data-driven insights into addiction, bridging the gap between computational linguistics and clinical substance abuse research, with significant i mplications f or c linical interventions. ©2025 IEEE.

title: Auto-Assess: Automated Evaluation Platform for Enhanced SQL learning
authors: Motiwala M.A.; Joshi A.; Chapaneri R.
venue: 2025 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science, SCEECS 2025
abstract: This work describes the designing and development of an AI-driven SQL learning and teaching platform that could be used for automating SQL query generation, evaluation, and feedback. Based on NLP and AI methodologies, the proposed platform makes use of Ollama's Llama 3 for schema extraction, question generation, and grading. For answer generation, a fine-tuned Llama 3 model on a variation of the spider dataset is used. MongoDB has been utilized for scalable data storage and React for an intuitive front-end interface. The platform enables an instructor to upload datasets and automatically generate SQL-based tests for students. Students are also given immediate feedback regarding their SQL queries, which otherwise is a labor-intensive task. The platform can also overcome challenges that traditional SQL education face, such as inconsistency in grading and delay in giving feedback, thereby offering personalized feedback, comprehensive performance reports, and engaging learners. Our solution would lighten the burden of teaching SQL by ensuring less effort from instructors while teaching their students dynamically, through the assessment of queries in a real-time setting. By automation in conjunction with the feature of real-time interactivity, this solution has been steered toward an effective and scalable learning experience for SQL by both educators and learners. © 2025 IEEE.

title: Using ChatGPT for assessment development in design and manufacturing engineering education: Opportunities and challenges
authors: Kumar V.
venue: International Journal of Mechanical Engineering Education
abstract: Recent advancements in Artificial Intelligence (AI) have created new opportunities to enhance teaching and learning in engineering education. Among these, ChatGPT, an AI language model developed by OpenAI, has gained significant popularity for its ability to generate human-like responses, explain technical concepts, and support problem-solving. While much of the current discourse focuses on student use of ChatGPT, its potential to assist instructors in designing assessments and providing feedback remains underexplored. This gap is particularly relevant in design and manufacturing engineering courses, where instructors often face challenges in crafting open-ended questions, programming-based assessments, and grading complex student submissions with consistency and clarity. To address this gap, this paper investigates the use of ChatGPT as a digital assistant to support assessment-related tasks in design and manufacturing engineering education. Using a reflective case study approach, the work evaluates ChatGPT across five key use cases: (i) Designing Multiple-Choice Questions, (ii) Designing Descriptive Questions, (iii) Designing Computational and Analytical Questions, (iv) Designing Image and Computer Programming-Based Questions, and (v) Designing Rubrics for Grading. Outputs were assessed using a structured pedagogical framework grounded in course learning objectives and Bloom's Taxonomy to evaluate cognitive depth, relevance, and alignment with instructional goals. Findings indicate that ChatGPT can effectively generate relevant and diverse assessment items, reduce instructor workload, and support personalized feedback. However, limitations such as occasional inaccuracies, difficulty generating technical visuals, and the need for instructor oversight highlight the importance of critical evaluation. By outlining both opportunities and constraints, this study offers actionable insights for integrating ChatGPT tools into assessment practices in design and manufacturing engineering education. © The Author(s) 2025

title: Advances Smart Quality Assessment in Jaggery Powder Production Using IoT and Machine Learning- A Review
authors: Ingawale K.; Kalle S.; Raval V.; Khambalkar C.; Kadam G.
venue: 2025 International Conference on Future Technologies, ICFT 2025
abstract: Jaggery powder is a natural, unprocessed sweetener produced by evaporating sugarcane juice, and it is widely consumed due to its rich nutritional profile, including minerals such as calcium, iron, antioxidants and magnesium's. It is especially popular in semi-urban and rural markets across Asia and Africa and is increasingly gaining grip in global health-conscious food markets. However, despite its increasing demand, the jaggery production industry remains largely conventional, depending heavily on manual methods for boiling, extraction, drying and clarification. These hand operated processes often lead to significant differences in product quality, including variability in texture, color, moisture content, granulation, and purity. Moreover, the absence of standardized quality control mechanisms can result in health and safety concerns such as microbial contamination, adulteration with chemicals, and inefficient drying, which affects shelf life. To address these challenges, this research investigates a novel approach to embed real-time, non-invasive quality assessment technologies into the jaggery powder production process. We explore the application of smart technologies such as Internet of Things (IoT) sensors for continuous environmental and process monitoring, computer vision systems for visual grading of granule size and color, near-infrared (NIR) spectroscopy for chemical composition analysis, and machine learning (ML) algorithms for automated classification and prediction of quality parameters. This integrated, data-driven framework allows producers to monitor critical quality metrics at every stage from sugarcane juice extraction to drying and packaging and enabling immediate feedback and adaptive control measures. The proposed smart jaggery production system ensures enhanced traceability, better standardization across batches, and compliance with food safety standards. Preliminary Pilot testing conducted in a small-scale jaggery unit using 500 samples demonstrated significant improvements: CNN-based visual grading achieved 93% accuracy, NIR-assisted ML models predicted moisture Content with an RMSE of 0.38% and impurity detection reached 89% precision. These findings confirm the effectiveness and scalability of the proposed IoT ML framework for delivering safer and higher-quality jaggery powder for both domestic and international markets. © 2025 IEEE.

title: AI-Driven Transformation of Higher Education a Systematic Review
authors: Erlangga A.P.D.; Magdalena Y.
venue: Proceeding - 2025 4th International Conference on Creative Communication and Innovative Technology: Empowering Transformative MATURE LEADERSHIP: Harnessing Technological Advancement for Global Sustainability, ICCIT 2025
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is a powerful catalyst for change, fundamentally reshaping the landscape of higher education by altering traditional methods of teaching, learning, and administration. This study aims to provide a comprehensive overview of this AI-driven transformation by systematically reviewing recent scholarly literature. The object of this research is to identify, categorize, and analyze the primary applications and documented impacts of AI within higher education institutions. Employing a Systematic Literature Review (SLR) methodology guided by PRISMA principles, this study analyzes 62 peer-reviewed articles sourced from the Scopus database, published between 2021 and 2024. The results indicate a significant and accelerating global research interest in this domain, with key applications concentrated in academic support, personalized learning enhancement, automated assessment, and administrative efficiency. While the findings confirm that AI integration significantly enhances educational outcomes and operational effectiveness, they also reveal critical challenges. A prominent theme across the literature is the emergence of significant ethical concerns, including inequities in technology access, data privacy, and the potential for over-reliance on AI tools, which may hinder the development of critical thinking skills. In conclusion, while AI offers transformative potential for higher education, its successful and ethical integration is not guaranteed. The findings imply an urgent need for policymakers and institutional leaders to establish robust governance frameworks that balance technological innovation with pedagogical integrity and equity, ensuring that AI serves as a tool for positive and inclusive educational advancement. © 2025 IEEE.

title: IELTS and TOEFL Automated Essay Scoring - Hybrid NLP and ML Approach
authors: Mali A.; Argulwar M.; More N.; Bhmare C.; Magar H.; Mali M.
venue: 2025 IEEE Pune Section International Conference, PuneCon 2025
abstract: This paper presents an Automated Essay Scoring (AES) system with the application of artificial intelligence in the form of Natural Language Processing (NLP), Explainable AI (XAI), and machine learning in order to achieve reliable scoring of essays and constructivist feedback. While AES systems are typically developed on simple numerical predictions of grade based on straightforward proportions, this system endeavours to incorporate semantic embeddings based on architectures of transformers, TF-IDF vectors, and linguistic assistant features and thus preserve semantic richness, lexical diversity, and structural complexity. The design is in the form of a sequence in modularity and is comprised of preprocessing and feature extraction and inference by a neural network-based model and creation of feedback with score predictions meeting IELTS and TOEFL requirements and expanding practical application in the process. Experiments using the ASAP dataset revealed LightGBM to have remarkable performance and achieved a Quadratic Weighted Kappa (QWK) score of 0.7416 and a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 0.4899 and thus surpassed performance by XGBoost and Random Forest methods. Additionally, the system uses specially created rubrics and discourses on task response aspects, coherence, lexical resources, and grammatical accuracy to produce feedback, turning AES from a straightforward grader into an educational tool. The system is promoted as an AES framework and enables in-situ real-world use in standardised testing and classroom deployments by simultaneously meeting accuracy standards and maintaining transparency and usability on a large and studentcentered scale. © 2025 IEEE.

abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) has accessed in modern life through web search engines and mobile apps. AI-based Automated Essay Scoring (AES) is playing a significant role in educational field. One noteworthy innovation is the application of Natural Language Processing in automating the evaluation of examination papers. This paper report delves into a detailed study on the implementation and effectiveness of automatic exam paper evaluation using NLP techniques. Traditionally, exam paper evaluation is a crucial aspect of assessing students' performance, yet it is often conducted manually, leading to potential biases influenced by factors such as the evaluator's mood or their relationship with the student seriousness for evaluation. title: PaperBench: Evaluating AI’s Ability to Replicate AI Research
authors: Starace G.; Jaffe O.; Sherburn D.; Aung J.; Shern C.J.; Maksin L.; Dias R.; Mays E.; Kinsella B.; Thompson W.; Heidecke J.; Glaese A.; Patwardhan T.
venue: Proceedings of Machine Learning Research
abstract: We introduce PaperBench, a benchmark evaluating the ability of AI agents to replicate state-of-the-art AI research. Agents must replicate 20 ICML 2024 Spotlight and Oral papers from scratch, including understanding paper contributions, developing a codebase, and successfully executing experiments. For objective evaluation, we develop rubrics that hierarchically decompose each replication task into smaller sub-tasks with clear grading criteria. In total, Paper-Bench contains 8,316 individually gradable tasks. Rubrics are co-developed with the author(s) of each ICML paper for accuracy and realism. To enable scalable evaluation, we also develop an LLM-based judge to automatically grade replication attempts against rubrics, and assess our judge’s performance by creating a separate benchmark for judges. We evaluate several frontier models on PaperBench, finding that the best-performing tested agent, Claude 3.5 Sonnet (New) with open-source scaffolding, achieves an average replication score of 21.0%. Finally, we recruit top ML PhDs to attempt a subset of PaperBench, finding that models do not yet outperform the human baseline. We open-source our code to facilitate future research in understanding the AI engineering capabilities of AI agents. © 2025, ML Research Press. All rights reserved.

title: MCQs Generation Using Ensemble Model for Student Performance Assessment
authors: Madri V.; Meruva S.
venue: Advances in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning
abstract: Multiple-Choice Questions have a vital role in the educational assessment; they are a conve-nient and scalable way to have students engaged in learning on multiple subjects. However, until recently, these questions had to be created almost manually by people or a team of experts who wrote questions based on the specific learning objective. As creating the question bank in this way requires a lot of effort, it is still not always feasible to use only MCQs in the assessment. The automated generation of MCQ may free some time for educators or help to evaluate the understanding of students more accurately. Recent advancements in Natural Language Processing and machine learning have made it possible to generate questions automatically based on existing educational content. This paper proposes an Time Constraint Limited MCQs Generation using Ensemble Learning Model (TCL-MCQs-ELM) that will create MCQs. The ensemble model proposed with this paper has combined the strengths of different machine algorithms.It has integrated transformer models, basic rule-based algorithms, and neural networks models, which might generate questions of low quality if used alone. This method guarantees that the generated MCQs are not just correct based on the context but also are at an acceptable rate. Other lecture note forms that would apply this ensemble model to generate MCQs are books and online-based notes. The quality assessment of the ensemble model involves carrying out an experiment pick view questions were randomly selected, and then the generated MCQ group had to select which was generated by the ensemble model. The quality, as coherence and relevancy, was compared, and the outcomes illustrated that the quality of the ensemble model is comparable to the manual MCQ generation process. Therefore, the ensemble model’s quality is considerable to be used to assess students’ knowledge of the educational platform system. However, the advantages of using this ensemble model are numerous, including scalability, reduced human input, and mediation on many educational domains, and thus apply a great range of platforms, classroom-based or e-learning. © 2025 VijayaRaju Madri and Sreenivasulu Meruva.

title: Automatic Generation of High-Quality MCQs With LLMs for Artificial Intelligence Education
authors: So C.C.; Lee C.H.; Yang D.; Loh A.W.K.; Sze C.K.
venue: IEEE Access
abstract: Modern education demands meticulous design of teaching and learning materials to foster effective learning. Multiple-choice questions (MCQs) are widely used in education worldwide due to their high scalability, automated grading efficiency, and the ability to evaluate conceptual understanding across various topics. However, creating high-quality MCQs that match learning objectives accurately, balance different question types, Bloom’s revised taxonomy levels, difficulty levels, and avoid repetition of content is demanding and time-consuming even for experienced educators. Large Language Models (LLMs) with deep reasoning capabilities offer novel opportunities for automated MCQ generation. Creating high-quality Multiple-Choice Questions (MCQs) is a time-consuming challenge for educators. This paper introduces a sophisticated pipeline that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to automatically generate high-quality MCQs for Artificial Intelligence education. Our method employs a zero-shot prompting strategy with different question types to guide LLMs in creating questions. To ensure reliability and mitigate inaccuracies, the pipeline integrates a novel Chain-of-Verification (CoVe) methodology called Cross-CoVe to systematically validate the generated content. The generated MCQs of our pipeline are rigorously evaluated on MCQ quality, explanation quality, and degree of diversity in a case study in an Artificial Intelligence (AI) course. We generated 200 MCQs, and the results demonstrate that 94.5% of MCQs had a single, correctly identified answer, and 79% provided robust explanations for both the correct answer and the distractors. The pipeline also achieved excellent diversity, with 99% of questions being non-duplicates. Notably, our Cross-CoVe verification strategy proved highly effective, correctly identifying 63.6% of flawed questions—a statistically significant improvement (p = 0.002) that more than doubles the performance of a self-verification baseline (27.3%). Our work contributes to the intense research and discussion on AI-driven educational tools, highlighting the high potential of state-of-the-art LLMs with deep reasoning capabilities to assist rather than replace educators, especially in large-scale educational resource development. © 2013 IEEE.

title: Optimizing LLMs for Automated Essay Scoring: Finetuning and Systematic Prompting
authors: ElMassry A.M.; Zaki N.
venue: 19th IEEE International Conference on Application of Information and Communication Technologies, AICT 2025 - Conference Proceedings
abstract: Automated Essay Scoring (AES) is a critical component in modern educational assessment, offering a scalable, consistent, and objective solution to evaluate student writing, while reducing the time burden on educators. Traditional AES systems, which rely on statistical models, have struggled to capture the subjective and nuanced nature of human scoring. The advent of Deep Learning (DL) and pretrained models has advanced AES research with their ability to capture the contextuality and generate more accurate scores. Most recently, Large Language Models (LLMs), such as GPT-4, have shown great promise due to their ability to handle long text and generate human-like responses. However, their performance is highly dependent on prior training, which may limit the base model’s performance in specialized tasks. Finetuning plays a critical role in improving LLM adaptability to specific tasks, like AES, but it is computationally intensive. Another key factor influencing the LLM performance is the prompting strategy used to guide the model. In this study, we evaluate the performance of the base GPT-4.1 and two finetuned instances on AES tasks using two different datasets, the benchmark ASAP and a domain-specific Operating Systems dataset. Initial results showed that finetuning significantly improved model adaptability and scoring accuracy. To further enhance performance, we apply DSPy. This systematic self-improving prompting framework has not yet been explored in the AES context, and yielded additional improvement of 6-7% in scoring accuracy while saving manual work. These findings highlight the importance of finetuning for task-specific adaptation, the potential of systematic prompting in addressing LLMs challenges, and improving performance in AES tasks. © 2025 IEEE.

title: Ethical Challenges of AI Integration in Open University Online Education: A Multidimensional Analysis

abstract: Open universities have long been at the forefront of delivering accessible education across geographical barriers. The integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) into online learning environments promises to enhance personalized instruction, streamline administrative processes, and support automated assessments. However, rapid AI integration also brings ethical challenges such as data privacy breaches, algorithmic bias, accountability dilemmas, machine-generated misinformation, and an erosion of human-centric pedagogy. This paper provides a multidimensional analysis of these ethical challenges and discusses mitigation strategies from technical, policy, pedagogical, and global cooperation perspectives. title: A Machine Learning-based Approach for Automated Error Classification in Texts using BERT Embeddings and Ensemble Learning
authors: Puranikmath J.N.; Suresh Y.
venue: 16th International Conference on Advances in Computing, Control, and Telecommunication Technologies, ACT 2025
abstract: Classifying automated text error is crucial task in natural language processing (NLP), with significant applications in academic assessment, automated grading and linguistic error detection. Traditional rule-based and statistical models often fail to generalize well across diverse datasets due to limited feature representations. This study proposes a robust machine learning approach using Bidirectional Encoder Representation from Transformers (BERT) embeddings to enhance text classification accuracy. Employed an ensemble learning strategy by combining Support Vector Machine and Random Forest classifiers in a Voting Ensemble model, effectively capturing both linear and non-linear patterns in linguistic errors. The dataset used comprises multiple categories of text errors, including grammar, coherence and augmentation, ensuring a comprehensive evaluation. The experimental results demonstrate the efficacy of the approach, achieving a high validation accuracy and test accuracy. Furthermore, integrated hyperparameter tuning, class imbalance handling, and data augmentation techniques to improve generalization and model robustness. Comparative analysis with baseline models, including standalone SVM and deep learning architectures, highlights the superiority of the method. This research contributes to advancing NLP-driven automated feedback systems and provides insights to develop quick writing assistants in educational and professional domains. © Grenze Scientific Society, 2025.

title: End-to-End Architecture for English Reading and Writing Content Assessment Based on Prompt Learning
authors: Wu S.-Q.; Pang M.-Y.; Sun X.-M.; Wang X.-J.; Ji Y.-P.
venue: IEEE Access
abstract: With the acceleration of globalization, the use of English as an international language has become increasingly widespread, making the assessment of English reading and writing skills a key issue in the field of language education. However, traditional methods of English reading and writing content assessment rely on manually designed features, which struggle to effectively handle complex and diverse language structures. To address this issue, this study proposes an English reading and writing content assessment algorithm based on Prompt Learning, utilizing an end-to-end architecture enhanced with multi-scale attention mechanisms. The algorithm preprocesses the input English texts through the prompt learning framework and uses multi-scale attention mechanisms to improve the model's ability to capture features at different linguistic levels. Within an end-to-end architecture, the entire assessment process is automated, from text input to output of assessment results, eliminating the need for manually designed feature extraction steps. Experimental results show that the algorithm performs excellently on multiple English reading and writing assessment datasets, significantly enhancing the accuracy and efficiency of assessments and offering an effective solution for English reading and writing evaluations.  © 2013 IEEE.

title: Eye Tracking-Based Substitution of Human Feedback for Image Quality Assessment in Diffusion Models
authors: Gu Q.; Zhou Z.; Qi J.; Wang H.; Xu C.
venue: IEEE Access
abstract: As diffusion models rapidly evolve, the need to incorporate manual feedback into their retraining processes has become increasingly urgent. This paper proposes a novel framework, ED3PO, to address this limitation and improve the objectivity and efficiency of image quality assessment in diffusion model retraining. The framework integrates the D3PO diffusion model with eye-tracking-based deep learning to automate the evaluation of image quality. Eye-tracking experiments were conducted with 200 participants to collect visual attention metrics during paired comparisons of image quality. Seven machine learning models were subsequently trained, with the best-performing Transformer model used to capture the relationship between eye-tracking data and human image quality assessments. The effectiveness of ED3PO retraining was validated through computational metrics and eye-tracking experiments, and it was further applied to four additional domains. By incorporating eye-tracking into the diffusion model retraining process, this study enables the automated assessment of image quality and the generation of higher-quality images. Furthermore, it offers a reference framework and future research directions for applying eye-tracking technology in the development of diffusion models. © 2013 IEEE.

authors: Li C.; Zhang L.; Guo H.; Zheng Q.

abstract: According to the grading standards for middle school examinations, thought content and structural coherence are critical indicators of essay quality. Evaluating an essay’s coherence requires not only analyzing the semantic connections between sentences but also understanding the logical relationships between sentences and the overall structural layout of the essay. This understanding is essential for grasping the logical framework of long texts and generating complex documents. This paper presents our approach used in the NLPCC 2024 competition for evaluating essay sentence logic and generating feedback. For different tasks, we applied both traditional classification models and advanced large language models, leveraging pseudo-labels generated through semi-supervised learning and information provided by large models as auxiliary data, significantly enhancing overall model performance. title: Generative artificial intelligence in education: A topic-based bibliometric analysis
authors: Tao L.; Deng H.; Song Y.
venue: Educational Technology and Society
abstract: Information and communication technologies have transformed education, driving it towards intelligent teaching and learning. With the rise of generative artificial intelligence (AI), represented by tools such as ChatGPT, there is also a growing body of literature on generative AI in education. In this study, we searched the Scopus, ERIC, and Web of Science databases as well as the proceedings of the International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Education and the International Conference on Educational Data Mining. These searches yielded 1,158 papers that we subjected to topic modelling analysis, the Mann-Kendall trend test, and keyword analysis to comprehensively review the evolving trends and dynamics of generative AI in education, active publication sources and authors, major research topics in generative AI education, and potential directions for cross-thematic research. We analysed the trends of keywords and relevance during different sub-periods. Topic modelling enabled us to classify all abstracts of the reviewed papers into 12 topics and identify the tendency of each topic. The topics included predicting student performance, learning tutoring systems, generative AI and AI literacy, writing/essay automated grading, chatbot-based learning/assessment, gamified/game-based learning & AI learning environments, emotional engagement, automatic feedback, question generation, generative AI use in peer assessment, personalised recommendation system, and simulation based study environment. Finally, a heat map and a hierarchy cluster provided information about the correlations between topics and the potential integration of different research directions for generative AI in education, offering a reference for future research. © (2024), (International Forum of Educational Technology and Society). title: MINT: Meta-Learning for Intelligent Prompt Creation

abstract: Manual prompt engineering for Large Language Models (LLMs) is not scalable and does not adapt with task complexity. MINT provides a meta-learning solution that allows LLMs to create adaptive, context-sensitive prompts automatically from task description and user goals. With hierarchical user context encoding and iterative self-refinement, MINT supports quick few-shot adaptation and effective personalization across content types. title: QUEST-AI: A System for Question Generation, Verification, and Refinement using AI for USMLE-Style Exams
authors: Bedi S.; Fleming S.L.; Chiang C.-C.; Morse K.; Kumar A.; Patel B.; Jindal J.A.; Davenport C.; Yamaguchi C.; Shah N.H.
venue: Pacific Symposium on Biocomputing
abstract: The United States Medical Licensing Examination (USMLE) is a critical step in assessing the competence of future physicians, yet the process of creating exam questions and study materials is both time-consuming and costly. While Large Language Models (LLMs), such as OpenAIs GPT-4, have demonstrated proficiency in answering medical exam questions, their potential in generating such questions remains underexplored. This study presents QUEST-AI, a novel system that utilizes LLMs to (1) generate USMLE-style questions, (2) identify and flag incorrect questions, and (3) correct errors in the flagged questions. We evaluated this systems output by constructing a test set of 50 LLM-generated questions mixed with 50 human-generated questions and conducting a two-part assessment with three physicians and two medical students. The assessors attempted to distinguish between LLM and human-generated questions and evaluated the validity of the LLM-generated content. A majority of exam questions generated by QUEST-AI were deemed valid by a panel of three clinicians, with strong correlations between performance on LLM-generated and human-generated questions. This pioneering application of LLMs in medical education could significantly increase the ease and efficiency of developing USMLE-style medical exam content, offering a cost-effective and accessible alternative for exam preparation.  © 2024 The Authors.

title: A Hybrid XAI-LLM Approach for Generating Explainable Feedback in an Intelligent Technical Interview Simulator
authors: Wijethunga W.M.S.; Wijesuriya W.M.R.M.
venue: Proceedings of ICITR 2025 - 10th International Conference on Information Technology Research: Advancing Global Connectivity Through Emerging Information Technology Solutions
abstract: The utility of AI in interview or educational assessment is often undermined by its "black box"nature, where systems provide scores without the explanatory feedback essential for learning. This research addresses this challenge by designing and validating a comprehensive, multi-stage pipeline that translates a classification model's prediction into qualitative, pedagogical feedback. We build our methodology around a CatBoost classifier to classify answers, then use an Explainable AI (XAI) framework (SHAP) to diagnose it and generate a structured map of influential keywords. This diagnostic map guides a Large Language Model (LLM), which functions as a semantic synthesis engine to create actionable counterfactuals and conceptual summaries. A blind, comparative expert evaluation found that the feedback from our hybrid pipeline was overwhelmingly preferred, achieving a near-perfect accuracy rating of 4.93/5.00. The key contributions of this work are the validation of a novel, synergistic pipeline that utilizes XAI to guide a constrained LLM, as well as a practical methodology for transforming opaque model predictions into transparent, pedagogically valuable feedback. title: From Palette to Reasoning: Improving LLM’s Visual Recognition Capabilities in Children’s Coloring Tasks
authors: Tapia-Mandiola S.; Araya R.
venue: Lecture Notes in Networks and Systems
abstract: Coloring exercises are a powerful tool for teaching math and computational thinking in young students. But for teachers, grading these tasks is far more complex than checking multiple-choice answers. In earlier work, we suggested using Large Language Models to help with this process, yet current vision models often fail to recognize balls, boxes, or colors when many similar shapes are present. To tackle this, we propose a two-step method: first, we segment the important parts of the image, which include boxes with colored balls, and remove visual distractions, and then we give these cropped sections to a GPT-style model using a zero-shot prompt. This process leads to clear improvements in recognizing colors and objects, even in difficult conditions like poor lighting, shadows, partially colored shapes, and varied marker types—conditions that reflect how kids actually color and how teachers take photos. Our findings show that simple visual pre-processing greatly boosts the accuracy and clarity of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) when used in early education settings. © The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2025.

title: Linguistically Informed Essay Assessment Framework to Analyze Writing Style Vocabulary Usage and Coherence
authors: Sreela B.; Neelambaram B.; Adusumilli M.; Rautrao R.R.; Smerat A.; Orosoo M.; Swathi A.
venue: International Journal of Advanced Computer Science and Applications
abstract: Automated essay scoring (AES) has become an essential tool in educational technology, yet many existing approaches rely on black-box models that lack interpretability and adaptability across diverse prompts and writing styles. Conventional transformer-based AES systems demonstrate strong accuracy, but often fail to provide pedagogically meaningful feedback or generalize effectively in low-resource settings, limiting their practical applicability. The proposed COSMET-Net (Contrastive and Explainable Semantic Meta-Evaluation Network) addresses these limitations by integrating contrastive learning, meta-learning, and explainable AI to produce an adaptive and interpretable evaluation of academic essays. Essays are processed through text cleaning, tokenization, and lemmatization, and embeddings are generated using pretrained transformers such as BERT and RoBERTa. Contrastive learning distinguishes high- and low-quality essays, while a Contrastive Linguistic Regularization (CLR) layer aligns embeddings with linguistic properties, enhancing interpretability. Meta-learning enables rapid adaptation to novel prompts with minimal additional data. The explainable output module, employing attention visualization and SHAP values, provides detailed feedback on grammar, coherence, vocabulary richness, and readability. The framework was implemented in Python with PyTorch and Hugging Face Transformers and evaluated on the IELTS Writing Scored Essays Dataset. COSMET-Net achieved an accuracy of 92%, a recall of 93%, and an F1-score of 92%, surpassing existing models such as hybrid RoBERTa + linguistic features (F1-score 84%) and discourse + lexical regression (F1-score 88%). These results demonstrate that COSMET-Net delivers highly accurate, flexible, and linguistically interpretable assessments, providing a scalable solution for automated and pedagogically meaningful essay evaluation. © (2025), (Science and Information Organization). All Rights Reserved.

title: Explainable AI-Powered Writing Tutor for Academic English Learning and Improvement
authors: Jawaid A.; Almutlaq A.; Albouq S.; Alyas T.; Kaur P.; Abbas Q.; Ul Haq M.I.
venue: AISTEMEDU 2025 - 2025 International Conference on AI-Driven STEM Education and Learning Technologies, Proceedings
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) has radically changed the pattern of language learning and assessment in education in recent years. Automated Essay Scoring (AES) systems, in particular, have received a great deal of interest as scalable methods for assessing academic writing proficiency. Recent demand for accurate and explainable automatic evaluation tools have raised the importance of XAI in English education. This paper presents an explainable approach to enhancing academic English using the IELTS Writing Scored Essays Dataset. For predicting overall writing band scores, we combine state-of-the-art natural language processing (NLP) models - BERT, RoBERTa, and DistilBERT - with traditional machine learning classifiers such as Ridge Regression, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and XGBoost. Transformer-based models have been shown to be capable of acquiring contextual language representations, while hand-crafted ML baselines use textual and semantic features. The findings of the experiment indicate that RoBERTa exhibits the highest accuracy among NLP models (R 2 =0.91, MAE=0.21), but XGBoost performs better among ML methods (R 2 =0.92, MAE=0.20). Furthermore, XAI algorithms (SHAP and LIME) are used to interpret model predictions and identify the linguistic and structural variables that contribute most to scoring. The suggested framework not only advances the creation of open AI-based evaluation models but also transcends this by providing pedagogical practices grounded in equitable, scalable, and evidence-based approaches to the academic study of English language.  © 2025 IEEE.

abstract: Technology is, transforming the appearance of all the industries fast and the education industry is not an exception. Re-configurating the Significance of Smart Classroom Spaces In such a way, the interest of the paper, in turn, is to show how IoT and AI can be used in smart classrooms, and to what extent, to reconfigure the teaching-learning process and deliver a continuous learning experience in its entirety. Smart boards, projectors, tablets and sensors are tools that exist in the classroom and have communication with the Internet in the so-called Internet of Things, which provides opportunities to get real-time information and interact with it. Smart gadgets enable the tracking of attendance and enable lights to turn on and off and the climate control, which makes the classroom more effective and convenient. AI, in contrast, scours vast troves of learner data, then generates personal learning tracks, automatic evaluations and automatic tabbing systems. AI-powered adaptive learning systems work by analyzing student weaknesses and strengths, and providing personalized content and immediate feedback for improved understanding and retention. Advantages of smart classroom Collaboration and interactive learning are the key features of smart classroom. IoT-enabled smartboards allow teachers and students to interact with digital content in a more interactive way, making lessons more interesting. AIs based chatbots and virtual assistants guide students for queries, reducing dependence on instructors for common queries. Speech-to-text and text-to-speech AI technologies also help students with disabilities to access educational content, making education more inclusive. Also, the learning experience can be improved with the use of internet of things and artificial intelligence. Complete automated attendance trackers through facial recognition or RFID technology without any manual roll calls saving precious teaching time. AI powered analytics Institutions need AI powered technical expertise to track traces of welcome students, recognize alarming learners in real time, propose immediate measures to improve academic outcomes. Predictive analytics also facilitate strong resource planning, sound curriculum delivery, and better decision-making across the campus. In addition, remote learning and hybrid education are based on AI and the IoT, making education more flexible and accessible. © 2025 IEEE.

title: Integration of Intelligent Tutoring Systems with Machine Learning Models to Facilitate Personalized Instruction, Automated Assessment, and Real-Time Feedback in Higher STEM Education
authors: Madhala R.T.; Suri S.; Rahul N.; Rusum G.P.; Sagar Boda S.; Gupta D.

abstract: The rapid rise of STEM education has set the scramble to begin the adaptive instructional models that could satisfy the needs of various learner profiles and deliver the scaled and efficient outcomes. The present paper outlines how Intelligent Tutoring Systems (ITS) and machine-learning models could be used to offer selective education, automatic assessment, and practical recommendations in high-quality STEM education. The framework combines supervised models of predicting accuracy with unsupervised clustering of profiling learners and reinforcement learning of adaptive intervention. An experimental analysis of a typical sample of a repertoire of 1,200 STEM students across three academic centers was analyzed and involved such an outcome as problem-solving accuracy, response time, and assessment results. The findings showed a 22% improvement in forecasting accuracy, 15% enhancement in adaptive learning efficiency, and 18% higher automated assessment reliability compared to baseline systems. Almost 20% reduced connective lag was achieved using real-time feedback systems that measured the convergent score of learner proficiency. This finding confirms that hybrid ITS and machine learning can generate both a stable and scalable platform of personalized STEM learning that can ensure enhanced learning outcomes and more efficient instruction design.  © 2025 IEEE.

title: A Fuzzy-Based Subjective Answer Assessment Grading System Using Sentence Transformer
authors: Senthilkumar P.; Nandhini K.
venue: Lecture Notes in Networks and Systems
abstract: Evaluating student knowledge presents a challenge, and one simple and efficient evaluation method is question-answer. Various types of question-answer approaches are available to evaluate a student’s skills. A subjective question is the most relevant method to check all the levels of skills. However, evaluating subjective answers is another challenge, and manually doing this requires an expert who is good at score grading and requires a lot of our time. We propose an automatic subjective answer assessment grading system using a sentence transformer model to solve these difficulties. The proposed method works with two predefined models: the Jaccard approach for checking lexical similarity and the Cosine similarity for semantic similarity. And finally, our sentence transformer model is used to compare the contextual similarity. Combining these value-based hybrid techniques yields a promising score based on the fuzzy logic used to give a subjective answer assessment grading, and it outperforms certain baseline approaches. © The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Nature Singapore Pte Ltd. 2025.

title: Large Language Models for Automated Personalized Feedback in SQL Education: A Preliminary Study
authors: Utkan K.T.; Öztayşi B.
venue: Lecture Notes in Networks and Systems
abstract: It is widely acknowledged that timely, personalized feedback is necessary for effective and rapid learning, yet providing such feedback at scale remains a challenge in higher education. In this study, we investigate the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) to automate feedback provision within the context of a Database Management Systems (DBMS) course, and we propose a system designed for this purpose. We introduce a feedback mechanism that utilizes GPT-4o to evaluate the SQL queries students write in response to exam questions, generating detailed, individualized critiques of their answers along with confidence indicators. To assess the feasibility of this LLM-centric approach, we conducted a preliminary experimental study using real student exam data. The results show that the GPT-4o-based system can produce feedback closely aligned with instructor evaluations in many cases, offering a promising solution for scaling formative assessment. We outline the design of the system, present observations from the case study, and discuss the potential benefits and challenges of employing LLMs for educational feedback in real-world classroom settings. © The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2025.

title: Advancements in Deep Learning Techniques for Automatic Text Assessment
authors: Kalra N.; Singhal S.; Dhami R.; Biswas P.; Kuntal S.
venue: 2nd International Conference on Intelligent Systems for Cybersecurity, ISCS 2025
abstract: Evaluating free-text answers is one of the toughest challenges in education because manual grading is slow, inconstant and difficult to scale. As online learning grows, there is a clear need for fair and reliable AI-based assessment tools. This research introduces a system that uses the DeBERTa-v3-small transformer model to automatically score question-answer (QA) pairs. Instead of assigning fixed categories, our approach predicts continuous scores, making it closer to how humans grade. We trained the model on 40-50 QA pairs from various subjects, applying careful preprocessing and normalization for accuracy. The results show that our system performs better than traditional methods and even standard BERT-based models, achieving lower error rates. While it works well for factual and structured answers, it struggles with subjective or partially correct responses, indicating to the need for more explainable and fair AI tools. Overall, this work spotlight the promise of transformer-based regression models in making educational assessment more scalable, consistent, and unbiased while also indicating toward future improvements in multilingual, multi-modal, and transparent grading systems. © 2025 IEEE.

title: Debiasing AI in Education through Different Ethical Insights
authors: Kaul H.
venue: Handbook of Global Philosophies on AI Ethics: Toward Sustainable Futures
abstract: This chapter explores the intersection of artificial intelligence (AI) and education, focusing on the ethical challenges that arise as AI technologies become increasingly integrated into educational practices. AI offers significant advantages in personalized learning, automated grading, and data-driven decision-making. However, these benefits come with ethical concerns related to bias, fairness, privacy, transparency, and accountability. The chapter aims to address these concerns through an analysis of various ethical frameworks, providing a roadmap for how AI can be ethically implemented in educational settings. The chapter is structured around a specific framework which emphasizes fairness, inclusivity, transparency, accountability, and privacy (FIT-AP) as key ethical principles for AI deployment in education. The author highlights the risks of algorithmic and data biases, which can exacerbate existing inequalities, particularly when AI systems are trained on biased or unrepresentative data. To mitigate these risks, the chapter suggests regular audits, the use of diverse datasets, and greater transparency in AI decision-making processes. Through this analysis, this chapter underscores the importance of ongoing collaboration between educators, developers, and policymakers in creating AI systems that serve all students equitably. Ethical governance, transparency, and continuous monitoring are critical to ensuring that AI in education enhances learning opportunities while safeguarding students’ rights and promoting inclusivity. The chapter concludes with a call for more rigorous ethical oversight in AI development to prevent the reinforcement of biases and inequalities in educational settings. © 2026 selection and editorial matter, Naresh Singh and Ram B. title: Is generative AI ready to replace human raters in scoring EFL writing? Comparison of human and automated essay evaluation
authors: Topuz A.C.; Yıldız M.; Taşlıbeyaz E.; Polat H.; Kurşun E.
venue: Educational Technology and Society
abstract: Language teachers mostly spend much time scoring students’ writing and may sometimes hesitate to provide reliable scores since essay scoring is time-consuming. In this regard, AI-based Automated Essay Scoring (AES) systems have been used and Generative AI (GenAI) has recently appeared with its potential in scoring essays. Therefore, this study aims to focus on the differences and relationships between human raters (HR) and GenAI scores for the essays produced by English as a Foreign Language (EFL) learners. The data consisted of 210 essays produced by 35 undergraduate students. Two HR and GenAI evaluated the essays using an analytical rubric divided into the following five factors: (1) ideas, (2) organization and coherence, (3) support, (4) style, and (5) mechanics. This study found that there were significant differences between the scores given by HR and those generated by GenAI, as well as variations among the HR themselves; nonetheless, GenAI’s scores were similar across dual evaluations. It was also noted that GenAI’s scores were statistically significantly lower than those of HR. On the other side, it was found that HR scores correlated weakly, while GenAI scores correlated strongly. A significant correlation was observed between HR-1 and GenAI across all factors, whereas the second HR-2 showed significant correlations with GenAI in only three factors. Therefore, this study can guide EFL teachers on how to reduce their workload in writing assessments by giving GenAI more responsibility in scoring essays. The study also offers many suggestions for future studies on AES based on the findings and limitations of the study. © Creative Commons CC-BY-NC-ND 3.0 license (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/3.0/).

title: AI and Higher Education
authors: Paruthi M.; Rani S.; Bohara S.; Gupta G.; Bhargava A.
venue: Chatbots and Beyond: Exploring the Future of Conversational Technology in Education
abstract: This chapter provides insight into the role of artificial intelligence (AI) in higher education in the context of Industrial Revolution 4.0 and Education 4.0. Appropriately embracing AI technology in higher education paves the way for critical thinking amongst the youth. These critical thinkers comprehending and working together with AI will surely lead to drastic changes in labor markets. The chapter incorporates social ontology and Latane's social impact theory to analyze the social consequences of advancements in AI. AI has allowed personalized and efficient grading to students by using tools such as ChatGPT, indicating the growing trend of integrating AI in higher education. By applying theoretical frameworks such as socio–technical systems theory and paradox theory, the chapter highlights the complexities and contradictions of AI integration in education, emphasizing the need for a balanced approach to address these challenges while advancing educational innovation. © ISTE Ltd 2025.

title: AI-Based System for Assessing and Ranking Code Efficiency
authors: Dabhade S.A.; Borate V.; Bhavsar A.; Nathankar S.; Patil P.; Das R.
venue: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Blockchain and Distributed Systems Security, ICBDS 2025
abstract: The significance of performance and correctness evaluations in automated assessment systems and competitive programming has now an important role to play. Indeed, the traditional evaluation frameworks have considered correctness and not speed, memory usage and complexity of the code. The paper provides an AI model that uses machine learning to rank and predict C++ code submissions. LOC, Cyclomatic Complexity, Execution Time, and Memory Usage, are some of the features obtained out of Project Codenet. A weighted formula of the structural complexity and resource consumption in a run time is the basis on which an efficiency score is determined. A Random Forest Regressor is fed on these scores to predict their values so that they are highly accurate in submission ranking. In order to make it easier to use, the system was designed with an interactive web-based UI that represents submission ranking, efficiency scores, and performance measurements. It provides the possibility of filtering by the problem ID, presenting trends in the form of graphs, and comparing the efficiency of the code to make it easier to locate solutions that are more adequate. This system can be assess on a real-time basis with restricted manual adjustment to scale to large-scale coding settings. The framework does not only have the capability of automating the evaluation of the code, but also can serve as a tutor to programmers and teachers, challenging them to code effectively. In the future, one will apply deep learning models to prediction accuracy and containerized environments (such as Docker) to provide more accurate benchmark results. The system proposed is going to be broadly applicable in training programs on programming, coding competitions and platform evaluation plans.  © 2025 IEEE.

title: AI Integration in Pedagogical Practices: A Qualitative Analysis of Teacher Training and Classroom Implementation
authors: Carvalho R.; Santos C.; Coelho A.; Ledesma F.
venue: Lecture Notes in Networks and Systems
abstract: Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) is transforming education by enabling personalized learning, automating assessment, and fostering creativity. However, its integration by educators remains underexplored. This study examines AI adoption among informatics teachers in Portugal through a qualitative thematic analysis of 125 project planning documents submitted in the ANPRI (Associação Nacional de Professores de Informática) Teacher Training Program, a national AI literacy initiative. Collected over three cohorts spanning two years, between 2022 and 2024, these documents reveal five key areas of AI adoption: digital storytelling and media creation, computational thinking and coding, automated assessment and feedback, ethical AI literacy and misinformation detection, and accessibility and personalized learning. Findings indicate that secondary educators lead AI adoption, particularly in computational thinking and assessment automation, while primary educators focus on creative applications such as storytelling. Vocational educators demonstrate moderate AI adoption, primarily for skill-based training. Sentiment analysis shows 40% of teachers view AI positively, citing engagement and efficiency benefits, while 30% express concerns regarding AI bias, misinformation, and assessment fairness. Word frequency analysis highlights recurring concerns, with terms such as “bias,” “assessment,” and “training” frequently appearing in negative reflections. These insights emphasize the need for structured AI literacy programs, transparent AI-based assessment policies, and ethical AI integration frameworks. This study provides empirical guidance for educators, policymakers, and AI developers to support responsible AI adoption in classrooms. © The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2025.

title: Stress Monitoring Using Wearable Technology
authors: Prasad S.; Upadhyaya V.
venue: International Conference on Computer and Applications, ICCA 2025 - Proceedings
abstract: The topic of this research is stress detection and management using wearable technology with the aim of making stress detection and management accessible and user-friendly. It focuses on helping users to easily track their stress levels and overall health. The review includes wearable devices that can be used for real-time stress detection, including those recommended by healthcare practitioners.There are numerous key findings from the research. Sensors like PPG, ECG, EDA are effective in measuring the physiological data that is needed for stress detection. For example, elevated heart rate and blood pressure, reduced HRV, lower skin temperature, and increased skin conductivityThere are also some gaps and challenges identified. First being the non-standardized grading system. Second is the subjective nature of stress. Third is the need for rigorous ML training to create personalized models. Another important problem to tackle is contextual awareness. The model should be able to differentiate between bodily changes caused by stress or other factors like exercise, caffeine etc.The future directions involve advancement in the current methodology with AI/ML based biofeedback mechanism that provides real-time calming feedback and also collects post intervention data to analyze its effectiveness. This proposal will help develop a personalized and adaptive model. © 2025 IEEE.

title: Automated Generation of Context-Aware Assessments using AI and NLP Techniques
authors: Sandana Karuppan A.; Hariharan M.; Sunthara Moorthy K.; Sashwin K.; Ramkumar T.R.
venue: International Conference on Software, Systems and Information Technology, SSITCON 2025
abstract: This paper presents an AI-powered system for the automatic generation of multiple-choice questions (MCQs) from diverse educational materials, addressing the need for efficient assessment creation in modern education. The system leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) as the primary engine for context-aware question generation, complemented by traditional Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques such as Named Entity Recognition and semantic preprocessing to refine extracted content and improve grammatical consistency. Experimental evaluation demonstrates that the concept extraction module achieves an accuracy of 92%, while question generation and validation modules maintain accuracies above 85%, ensuring high-quality assessments. The system efficiently processes documents at scale, generating 40 to 45 MCQs per 10 pages in under 12 seconds, making it suitable for real-time educational applications. Qualitative analysis via key concept density further confirms the system's ability to capture essential instructional content. Integration with Google Forms streamlines quiz deployment, reducing educator workload and enhancing assessment consistency. These results highlight the system's robustness, scalability, and pedagogical alignment, underscoring the transformative potential of LLM-driven quiz generation in enhancing teaching and learning experiences. © 2025 IEEE.

title: Natural Language Processing for Subjective Exam Grading in Programming Explanations in Thai Language
authors: Rattanimit W.; Nomponkrang T.
venue: Proceedings - iEECON 2025: 2025 13th International Electrical Engineering Congress: Carbon Neutrality: Challenges and Solutions Based on Sustainable Power of Nature
abstract: In the digital era, learning programming has become an essential skill, and assessing students' understanding in this area plays a crucial role in enhancing educational quality. However, evaluating subjective programming exam responses, especially those involving conceptual explanations or procedural descriptions, remains a challenging task due to the diversity and complexity of answers. Manual grading can lead to delays and inconsistencies in scoring. This research aims to develop an automated system for evaluating Thai subjective programming exams using Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques to assess the correctness of responses. The system is implemented in Python, utilizing the ThaiNLP library for word tokenization, alongside Machine Learning algorithms such as Naive Bayes, Decision Tree, and Support Vector Machine (SVM) for classification and prediction of answers. The experimental dataset includes responses from 120 students who answered subjective questions about Python commands. Expert testing indicated high user satisfaction with an average score of 4.05. The findings show that SVM achieved the highest accuracy at 85.33%. This system can enhance efficiency and consistency in exam evaluation, reducing grading time and contributing to standardized scoring. © 2025 IEEE.

title: EDUCATIONAL ASSESSMENT IN THE AGE OF AI: A NARRATIVE REVIEW ON DEFINITIONS AND ETHICAL-LEGAL PRINCIPLES FOR TRUSTWORTHY AUTOMATED SYSTEMS
authors: Boccuzzi G.; Manganello F.; Nico A.

abstract: This study presents a narrative review of scholarly literature at the intersection of education, explainable artificial intelligence (XAI), and automated assessment algorithms. Acknowledging the pressing need for a unified regulatory framework to enable the safe deployment of AI-based assessment technologies in educational environments, the paper investigates whether a coherent and consistent terminology is employed across academic research. It further compiles and systematizes the ethical and legal principles underpinning the use of such systems, from the Ethical "AI explainability" to the emergent "Right to Explanation" introduced by Article 22 of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). The review finds that key concepts such as "assessment" and "explainability" are currently used with divergent meanings across disciplines. The analysis identifies transparency, accountability, fairness, privacy, and non-discrimination as the core principles most frequently emphasized in the literature, highlighting the need for interdisciplinary convergence to ensure trustworthy and equitable AI integration in Education. © 2025 IADIS Press All rights reserved.

title: Opportunities and considerations for using artificial intelligence in bioinformatics education
authors: Piccolo S.R.; Nathan A.; Brazas M.D.; Kandpal M.; Miró-Herrans A.T.; Kleinschmit A.J.; McClatchy S.; Mutheiwana P.; Nikolic D.; Gallo L.I.; Julius R.S.; Lloret-Llinares M.; Mulder N.; Presgraves D.; Shewaramani S.; Xool-Tamayo J.; Chain F.J.J.; Arantza Sanchez Guerrero S.
venue: Bioinformatics Advances
abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) tools and techniques are undoubtedly being used in bioinformatics education, reflecting broader trends in education. However, many instructors and learners may be unaware of the full scope of potential uses for these tools within bioinformatics education, as well as effective practices for using them. Building on discussions held at the 6th Global Bioinformatics Education Summit, this perspective article provides insights about ways that AI might be used to generate or adapt instructional content, provide personalized help for learners, and automate assessment and grading. Additionally, we highlight AI skills that are important for bioinformatics learners to develop in order to effectively use AI as a bioinformatics learning tool. We highlight currently available tools in the quickly evolving AI landscape and suggest ways that instructors or learners might use such tools. Furthermore, we discuss key considerations and challenges associated with integrating AI into bioinformatics education, including ethical implications, potential biases, and the need to critically evaluate AI-generated content. Finally, we highlight the need for further research to better understand how AI tools are being used in practice and empower their effective and responsible use in bioinformatics education. © The Author(s) 2025.

title: AI-Driven Grading and Moderation for Collaborative Projects in Computer Science Education
authors: Yu S.; Zagula A.
venue: Proceedings of the International Multi-Conference on Society, Cybernetics and Informatics, IMSCI
abstract: Collaborative group projects are integral to computer science education, fostering teamwork, problem-solving, and industry-relevant skills. However, assessing individual contributions within group settings is a long-standing challenge. Traditional assessment strategies, such as equal distribution of grades or subjective peer assessments, fall short in terms of fairness, objectivity, and scalability, especially in large classrooms. This paper introduces a semi-automated, AI-assisted grading system that evaluates both project quality and individual effort using repository mining, communication analytics, and machine learning models. The system comprises modules for project evaluation, contribution analysis, and grade computation, integrating seamlessly with platforms like GitHub. A pilot deployment in a senior-level course demonstrated high alignment with instructor assessments, increased student satisfaction, and reduced instructor grading effort. We conclude by discussing implementation considerations, ethical implications, and proposed enhancements to broaden applicability. © 2025 Proceedings of the International Multi-Conference on Society, Cybernetics and Informatics, IMSCI.

title: ASD-HI: A Parent-Child Interaction Dataset for Automated Assessment of Home Intervention
authors: Li Z.; Akemoglu Y.; Lyu J.; Zheng Q.; Xiong J.
venue: Lecture Notes in Computer Science
abstract: Home-based interventions are vital for supporting young children with autism spectrum disorder (ASD), yet many parents struggle to implement strategies effectively due to limited training. While specialists such as educators and speech-language pathologists provide guidance, real-time feedback outside professional settings remains scarce. To bridge this gap, we leverage advances in AI to support parents through automated assessment. However, training such AI systems requires robust data, which is currently limited. To address this, we created ASD-HI (Autism Spectrum Disorder - Home Intervention), a multi-modal dataset comparing 473 real parent-child interaction videos across three families. ASD-HI supports two core tasks: 1) Strategy Detection, identifying the behavioral strategies parents use, and 2) Fidelity Assessment, assessing the fidelity with which these strategies are implemented. We also propose a prompting-based LLM pipeline as a reference approach. It achieves 74% recall and 50% precision for strategy detection and 60% accuracy for fidelity assessment. Our work lays a foundation for developing AI-driven tools to enhance home interventions and improve outcomes for children with special needs. © The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2025.
